title: Success, belonging and identity work among care-experienced academics in the United Kingdom
abstract: Significant research and policy attention is now given to the social diversity of the academic workforce in the United Kingdom. One group that has not previously been foregrounded are those academics who are 'care-experienced' - i.e. who spent time in the care of the state in childhood, which is frequently associated with educational disruption and other challenges. This empirical article draws on semi-structured interviews with 21 care-experienced academics, focusing on themes around success and belonging within their career. It is framed by the concept of 'identity work', which seeks to understand the cognitive, emotional, social and physical tasks associated with maintaining and projecting desired identities. We conclude that care-experienced academics are engaged in substantial identity work, negotiating between their care-experienced and academic identities. In particular, many were navigating questions about whether to disclose their experience of care to colleagues and students, identifying benefits alongside concerns about stigma and stereotyping.
authors_text: Harrison, Neil; Benham-Clarke, Simon; Yeomans, Lucy
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
